coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for selective detection of TNT and TNP explosives in aqueous medium through fluorescence emission enhancement mechanism

coredata.dc:description: Due to rising threats of terrorism, pollution, timely and rapid detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) and 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) explosives has earned paramount significance. Herein, easily synthesized fluorenone sensor 3 was found selective towards the detection of TNP with a limit of detection (LOD) value of 0.5 nM. The fluorescence detection of TNP was achieved through the electrostatic interaction between sensor and TNP. Moreover, sensors 4 and 5 were rationally synthesized for selective nanoscale detection of TNT (LODs; 5.0 nM (4) and 7.0 nM (5)) through fluorescence emission enhancement response. Selective detection of TNT was accomplished through blockage of ICT and ESIPT in sensors. Sensing mechanisms were supported by 1H NMR titration, Job’s plots, computational methodologies, and DLS analysis. Interestingly, sensor 3 also showed selective colorimetric response towards TNP while sensors 4 and 5 displayed selective colorimetric detection of TNT explosive. Explosive responsive test kits were also prepared for on-site detection of TNP by sensor 3 and TNT by sensors 4 and 5. Moreover, sensors 3, 4, and 5 were successfully applied in the analysis of industrial effluents for the rapid detection of explosives.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for selective detection of TNT and TNP explosives in aqueous medium through fluorescence emission enhancement mechanism

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to rising threats of terrorism, pollution, timely and rapid detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) and 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) explosives has earned paramount significance. Herein, easily synthesized fluorenone sensor 3 was found selective towards the detection of TNP with a limit of detection (LOD) value of 0.5 nM. The fluorescence detection of TNP was achieved through the electrostatic interaction between sensor and TNP. Moreover, sensors 4 and 5 were rationally synthesized for selective nanoscale detection of TNT (LODs; 5.0 nM (4) and 7.0 nM (5)) through fluorescence emission enhancement response. Selective detection of TNT was accomplished through blockage of ICT and ESIPT in sensors. Sensing mechanisms were supported by 1H NMR titration, Job’s plots, computational methodologies, and DLS analysis. Interestingly, sensor 3 also showed selective colorimetric response towards TNP while sensors 4 and 5 displayed selective colorimetric detection of TNT explosive. Explosive responsive test kits were also prepared for on-site detection of TNP by sensor 3 and TNT by sensors 4 and 5. Moreover, sensors 3, 4, and 5 were successfully applied in the analysis of industrial effluents for the rapid detection of explosives.

ce:section.ce:para: Explosives are chemical substances that liberate surplus heat and hazardous gases through decomposition when triggered by thermal and chemical stimuli [1,2]. Among various explosives, nitroaromatic compounds (NACs) such as 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) are recognized as very dangerous explosives and are extensively utilized in military and terroristic activities. It posed serious security and health concerns globally [3]. TNP and TNT possess more explosive capacity in comparison to other comparable nitroaromatic explosives that led to their large-scale utilization in the military until World War I [4,5]. Explosives, particularly TNP, are exploited in pharmaceuticals, electric batteries, metal etching, rocket fuel, pesticides, and textile industries [6]. Similarly, TNP and TNT residues in military areas contaminate natural resources like ground water supplies and soil that lead to the severe health issues in animals and humans [7]. The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) perceived TNP and TNT as potential carcinogens and warned that their excessive exposure may damage organs like liver and kidney [8,9]. Since, toxicity of TNP and TNT explosives is an extreme threat to mankind. Hence, TNP or TNT contaminated areas should be identified immediately and strict measures need to be taken for their immediate removal. In recent years, sensitive and selective detection of TNT and TNP has become crucial due to their increased threat to human life and national security. Different techniques and convergent approaches have been potentially adopted in recent past to detect these explosives such as GC–MS [10], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [11], electrochemical method, X-ray spectrometry [12] high performance liquid chromatography [13], and mass spectrometry [14]. However, various drawbacks and limitations are associated with these techniques such as time taking, operational difficulties, inadequate portability and high cost that make them unsuitable for highly selective and sensitive on-site detection of TNP and TNT explosives. Therefore, cost-effective and easy-to-operate analytical methods are highly appealing. In this regard, fluorescence detection has emerged as a promising analytical detection technique owing to its high selectivity, extreme sensitivity, better portability, easy operation, and low cost. These advantages make fluorescence-based technique exceptionally appropriate to detect traces of TNP and TNT explosives. Huge efforts have already been made to develop fluorescent sensors that can be applied for an efficient sensing of TNP and TNT. These sensors include small organic probes [15], polymers [16], quantum dots [17], and metal organic framework (MOF) [18]. Most of the currently reported TNP and TNT sensors usually exhibit fluorescence turn-off (quenching) behavior upon interaction with these explosives. Fluorescence quenching occurs when there is an energy transfer or electronic transition from electron rich fluorescent molecule to the electron deficient nitroaromatics based on photoinduced electron transfer (PET), Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT), and charge transfer complexation [19–21]. Nonetheless, several disadvantages are associated with fluorescence quenching such as photobleaching, background fluorescence, excited state interaction, false signals, and self-quenching [22–25]. Most of the quenching based fluorescence sensors exhibit a monotonic and uneven decrease in emission intensity that hamper their sensing ability. Thus, fluorescence enhancement based sensors are highly desirable and became reliable alternate to overcome these limitations. In fluorescence enhancement based sensors, the addition of electron deficient explosive restricts non-radiative energy decay pathways like excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Moreover, practical applicability of fluorescence enhancement based sensors for on-site detection of TNP and TNT explosives in aqueous medium is prerequisite. To the best of our knowledge, fluorescence enhancement based TNP and particularly TNT sensors with real sample applications in aqueous media have not been reported yet in the literature.

In continuation of our research on the development of fluorescent sensors [26–36], fluorescence enhancement based sensors 3, 4, and 5 have been developed that are employed for sensitive and selective detection of TNP and TNT explosives in aqueous medium. Amino fluorenone sensor 3 was successfully employed for selective and sensitive detection of TNP explosive through fluorescence emission enhancement. This is attributed to the electrostatic interaction between acidic hydroxyl unit of TNP and amino group of sensor 3 which fulfills the criteria of efficient binding. Such interactions are well documented by Bedanta and co-workers where an amino group of curcumin-tryptophan conjugate develops a selective interaction with picrate ion but sensitivity of reported compounds is quite low (detection limit, 13.5 nM) in comparison to sensor 3 (0.5 nM) [37]. Interaction of sensor 3 with TNP blocks its non-radiative decay pathway i.e. photoinduced electron transfer (PET) as a result of their electrostatic interaction that induces fluorescence enhancement. Moreover, sensors 4 and 5 selectively detected TNT explosive by fluorescence enhancement response that was accomplished through the blockage of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT). It is believed that the design of ICT and ESIPT based sensors requires strong electron donating and electron withdrawing group/s within the same compound [38,39]. Keeping this in view, electron donating moiety (–OH, receptor) was introduced in TNT sensors 4 and 5 by installing hydroxyl/s substituted suitable aromatic rings through Schiff base reaction. An electrophilicity of carbonyl carbon in fluorenone scaffold (fluorophore) provokes the ICT and ESIPT within sensors 4 and 5. The ICT and ESIPT occur only when HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) of the receptor is located at higher energy as compared to the HOMO energy level of fluorophore. These kind of sensors display fluorescence enhancement upon binding with electron deficient explosive. It is anticipated that TNT bound receptor in sensors 4 and 5 lowers its HOMO energy level in comparison to the HOMO of excited fluorophore that induce stability in sensors. This stability in HOMO level of the bound receptor inhibits ICT and ESIPT pathways that leads to the rigidity of sensors and ultimately results in enhancement in fluorescence emission of sensor. It is thus worthwhile to mention that easily synthesized sensor 3 displayed selective detection of TNP with LOD 0.5 nM while sensors 4 and 5 exhibited selective sensing of TNT explosive with detection limits of 5.0 nM and 7.0 nM in aqueous medium using fluorescence enhancement response. Thus, sensors (3, 4, and 5) reported in this study exhibited more sensitive and selective response towards nitroaromatic explosives in comparison to already reported explosive sensors [40–44].

The structural characterization of sensors 3, 4, and 5 was performed by 1H and 13C NMR spectroscopy. The photophysical properties were investigated through UV–Visible and fluorescence emission measurements. Sensing mechanisms were validated through 1H NMR titrations, dynamic light scattering (DLS), density functional theory (DFT) calculations, and Job’s plots. Further, sensors reported in this study provided linear response for quantification of selective nitroaromatic explosives. Sensors displayed visual detection of TNP and TNT explosive in daylight and under UV. Further, TNP and TNT responsive test kits were also prepared that provided selective on-site detection of these explosives. In addition to this, sensors 3, 4, and 5 were successfully applied in the analysis of industrial wastes for rapid detection of explosives.
Instruments, reagents, synthetic procedures, and the detail of fluorescence experiments for sensors 3, 4, and 5 are presented in supplementary information (S1–6).

A three-step synthetic strategy was adopted to develop fluorenone functionalized Schiff base chemosensors 3, 4, and 5 as depicted in Scheme 1 . Nitration of 9H-fluorenone 1 followed by SnCl2 mediated reduction of nitro group provided 2-amino-9H-fluorenone 3 in good yield. Next, the reaction of amino-substituted fluorenone 2 with salicylaldehyde and 2,3-dihydroxybenzaldehyde in methanol afforded the fluorophores 4 and 5, respectively. Structural characterization of synthesized compounds 4 and 5 was performed by NMR spectroscopy. The NMR spectra of all synthesized compounds are provided in supplementary information (S32-S75).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sensors’ concentration and conditions for sensing studies
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption and fluorescence spectra of sensors 3, 4, and 5 (40 µM) were obtained in a variety of solvents including water, THF, ethanol, CH3CN, DMF, DMSO, toluene, and ethylene glycol to evaluate the effect of solvents on their absorption and emission properties (Fig. S1). It is well known that viscosity and polarity of a solvent may affect the absorption and emission wavelength of a sensor [45]. Sensors displayed comparable absorption and emission response in all mentioned solvents (Fig. S1). Slight changes in absorption or emission wavelength is presumably due to difference in the dissolution of sensors in different solvents. Results revealed that significant absorption and emission of sensor 3 were observed in water. Similarly, sensors 4 and 5 exhibited exquisite emission in THF. Further, the emission spectra of sensors 4 and 5 were recorded by adding different water fractions (f w) to their THF solution. Interestingly, it was noticed that the addition of different water fractions (f w) did not influence the emission intensity and emission wavelength (λmax) of sensors. It shows that sensors 4 and 5 didn’t aggregate at higher water contents and displayed high emission in H2O/THF (7:3, v/v) solvent system (Fig. S2). It is well known that different Schiff bases are susceptible and pose serious concerns about their stability in the aqueous medium. Keeping decomposition possibility of sensors 4 and 5 in mind, their stability was investigated in aqueous solution (H2O/THF (7:3, v/v). The emission bands of sensors 4 and 5 centered at 511 and 561 nm remained undistracted when scanned after regular interval of ten days (Fig. S3). The stability was also analyzed by measuring zeta potential of sensors 4 and 5 in their aqueous media (H2O/THF (7:3, v/v) as shown in Fig. S4. In this regard, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis of sensors 4 and 5 in aqueous medium (H2O/THF (7:3, v/v) was carried out after 10 days of their solution preparation. The potential values were found to be 13.51 and 14.23 mV for 4 and 5, respectively that correspond to the excellent stability of sensors in the aqueous medium (H2O/THF (7:3, v/v).

Based on above experimental results, water was chosen as a suitable solvent for sensor 3. Moreover, H2O/THF (7:3, v/v) system was selected for sensors 4 and 5 to execute absorption and emission studies. Net, the fluorescence experiments were carried out to investigate the concentration at which sensors 3, 4, and 5 exhibit the maximum emission. Sensors displayed different fluorescence emission intensities (λmax) at different concentrations. Since, weak fluorescence emission of a fluorophore is observed owing to inner filter effect when fluorophore concentration is used beyond a certain limit [46]. Therefore, optimization for maximum emission intensity was intended to avoid the inner filter effect. For this purpose, various concentrations of sensors 3, 4, and5 from 10 to 200 µM were scanned to investigate concentration of maximum emission intensity. It was noted that sensors3, 4, and5displayed the highest emission at 40 µM each with an emission wavelength of 447, 511, and 561 nm, respectively (Fig. S5). Comparison of emission wavelengths of sensors 3, 4, and5suggests that sensor4is red shifted as compared to sensor3 while sensor 5 is red shifted in comparison to sensor 4 (Fig. S6). Further, other experimental conditions including pH, buffer, and time for were also optimized and kept constant during sensing studies. The fluorescence sensitivity can be easily affected by varying pH of a system and ultimately sensitivity of sensor is compromised [47]. In this regard, influence of pH on the fluorescence signals of sensors3,4, and5was evaluated by varying pH values from 2.0 to 12.0 (Fig. S7A). Negligible fluctuations in fluorescence signals were observed when pH was changed between 7.0 and 10.0. However, disturbance in fluorescence signal of sensor 3 (40 µM) was noticed in acidic (pH below 5.0) and fluorescence signals of sensors 4 and5experienced slight reduction in basic medium (pH above 10.0). This is presumably due to the protonation of amino group of sensor3in the acidic medium and hydroxyl de-protonation of sensors4and5in the basic medium. The results suggested that the best fluorescence spectra were recorded at pH 10.0 in the respective aqueous media of each sensor and was chosen as an optimum pH for fluorescence sensing studies (Fig. S7A). Similarly, the addition of different buffer systems can affect the fluorescence signals that may alter sensitivity of a sensor. In this regard, different buffers were investigated to attain an optimum fluorescence response of a sensor in a suitable buffer. Intense fluorescence signals were obtained with the Britton–Robinson (B-R) buffer system at optimum pH 8.0. Conclusively, 0.2 M B-R buffer was selected as an optimized concentration to carry all fluorescence studies (Fig. S7B). Other important parameter to get the best spectrofluorometric results is the investigation of time during which a sensor makes the best possible interaction with an analyte. Therefore, each sensor (40 µM) was mixed with 30 nM of respective analyte (sensor 3with TNP;4and5with TNT), and relative fluorescence signals were recorded after different time intervals between 0 and 60 s (Fig. S8). Sensors 3, 4, and5displayed significant fluorescence enhanced signals at 25 sec., 18 sec., and 30 sec. respectively that shows that sensors displayed quick response towards specific analyte (sensor 3towards TNP;4and5towards TNT).

The UV–Vis. spectra of sensors 3, 4, and 5 in aqueous solution (100% H2O (3) and H2O/THF (7:3, v/v) (4 and 5)) exhibited absorption bands at 327, 351, and 391 nm, respectively corresponding to their n → π* transitions (Fig. S9). Similarly, sensor 3 displayed weak blue emission at 447 nm with a Stokes shift of 120 nm due to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from its amine unit (Fig. S9). Weak green emissions of sensors 4 and 5 was obtained at 511 and 561 nm that are attributed to the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process. Large Stokes shift of 160 and 170 nm in sensors 4 and 5 is a strong indication of ICT process. Notable bathochromic shift in absorption and emission spectra of sensors 4 (24 and 64 nm) and 5 (64 and 114 nm) as compared to that of sensor 3 is due to their additional hydroxyl units and increased π conjugation. Further, sensor 5 is relatively better emissive in comparison to sensor 4 that is presumably due to intramolecular hydrogen bonding in vicinal hydroxyl units of sensor 5. The photophysical properties of sensors 3, 4, and 5 are summarized in Table 1 .
It is anticipated that the photophysical properties of sensors 3, 4, and 5 can be tuned by interruption of PET and ICT processes through an electron deficient analyte.

To estimate selectivity of sensor 3, titration experiments were carried out against other nitroaromatics including TNT, DNT, NP, DNB, and NA. Fluorescence enhancement behavior of sensors 3 (40 μM) with other targeted NACs (0–100 nM) is presented in Fig. S10. Upon addition of increasing concentrations of NP (0–100 nM) to aqueous solution (100% H2O) of sensor 3, noticeable enhancement (10 folds) in fluorescence emission of sensor 3 was observed as shown in Fig. S10A. The calculated detection limit (48 nM) and binding association value (5.4 × 103 M−1)

of sensor 3 for 4-nitrophenol (NP) are quite low as compared to that for TNP (0.5 nM). Similarly, sensor 3 displayed less fluorescence enhancement response towards continuous addition of TNT, DNT, NA, and DNB (0–100 nM) with less Ka values (4.7 × 103, 3.8 × 102, 3.5 × 102, and 2.29 × 102 M−1) and low detection limits (61, 72, 75, and 78 nM, respectively). Depending upon the calculated Ka values and detection limits, fluorescence enhancement response of sensor 3 towards nitroaromatics were found to be in the order of TNP > NP > TNT > DNT > NA > DNB. Binding associations (Ka) and detection limits of sensor 3 for targeted NACs is tabulated in Table S1. A strong association of sensor 3 with TNP can be anticipated from the above experimental data. Sensor 3 experienced a substantial enhancement in its fluorescence emission (91.3%) in the presence of TNP that could mainly be attributed to suppression of the PET process in sensor 3. Less enhancement efficiency of sensor 3 with remaining NACs is due to insignificant complexation of sensor with targeted nitroaromatics (Fig. 2 A). Keeping these results in view, the Benesi–Hildebrand 3D column was plotted to evaluate the selectivity of sensors 3 (40 μM) towards nitroaromatic explosives (0–100 nM) (Fig. 2B). Pronounced fluorescence enhancement and favored interaction of sensor 3 was noticed only for increasing concentration of TNP (0–100 nM). Furthermore, significant change in color of sensor 3 was only observed when TNP was added to sensor 3 (under UV, 365 nm). But no color change was noticed against all other targeted nitroaromatic explosives (Inset; Fig. 2B). These results clearly highlight remarkable selectivity of sensor 3 for TNP among other targeted NACs.

Excellent selective fluorescence response of sensor 3 for TNP was also supported by UV–Vis. spectroscopy. In this regard, upon addition of nitroaromatic explosives (50 nM) to sensor 3 (40 μM), enhancement in absorbance of sensor 3 was only observed against TNP. However, addition of other targeted NACs did not produce any notable spectral change in absorption of sensors 3 (40 μM) as shown in Fig. S11A. These results revealed the excellent selectivity of sensor 3 for detection of TNP. Similar pattern was observed through naked eye colorimetric experiment that displayed selective change in yellow color of sensor 3 (500 nM) to red in the presence of TNP (10 μM) (Inset; Fig. S11A). Further, detailed UV–Vis. study of sensor 3 was carried out against selective TNP explosive. Upon progressive addition of TNP (0–50 nM) to aqueous solution of sensor 3, absorption band of sensor 3 showed enhancement in absorption band at 327 nm. This spectral change (enhancement) ascertains electrostatic interaction between sensor 3 and TNP explosive. Further, linear fit plot of sensor 3 + TNP was exploited that displayed linear relation between hyperchromic shift of sensor 3 and increasing concentrations of TNP (Fig. S12). Binding association (Ka) of sensor 3 with TNP was estimated through linear fit plot and was found to be 4.89 × 107 M−1 which accounts for efficient interaction between sensor 3 and TNP. In summary, all these spectral results infer the excellent sensitivity and selectivity of sensor 3 for TNP that is attributed to blockage of the PET.

Excellent selectivity of sensor 3 towards TNP can also be accomplished through validating their interaction energies. Interaction energies for the complexes of sensor 3 with targeted nitroaromatic explosives were obtained through calculations at Gaussian 09 program using B3LYP/6-31G(d) methodology [55]. Optimized complexes of sensor 3 with targeted nitroaromatics are given in Fig. S13. Obtained results reveal that TNP@3 has −8.93 kcal/mol interaction energy that is higher than all other relevant complexes. Interaction energies of sensor 3 with remaining NACs (NP@3, DNT@3, TNT@3, NA@3 and DNB@3) are −8.01, –8.01, −6.58, −5.89, −5.33, and −5.18 kcal/mol, respectively. The interaction energies trend of sensor 3 against nitroaromatic explosives is TNP > NP > DNT > TNT > NA > DNB. Keeping in view all the spectral changes and thermodynamic results, it is concluded that sensor 3 is selective towards detection of TNP.

Since, sensor 3 displayed good selectivity and excellent sensitivity towards TNP. It was highly fascinating to exploit sensor 3 as a core structure for the development of highly conjugated fluorescence sensors with significantly large Stokes shift. In this study, sensors 4 and 5 were developed from sensor 3 while retaining and enhancing sensors’ characteristic features, that are, good sensitivity, large Stokes shift, and high fluorescence enhancement response. Addition of suitable aromatic ring along with hydroxyl units to sensors 4 and 5 were expected to further tune their photophysical properties. Keeping this in perspective, highly conjugated and electron rich sensors 4 and 5 were designed and used for the sensing of electron deficient nitroaromatic explosives including TNT, TNP, DNT, NP, DNB, and NA through fluorescence and UV − Vis. spectroscopy under optimized experimental conditions. In this context, fluorescence experiments were carried out in aqueous solution (H2O/THF (7:3, v/v) in the presence of varying concentrations of nitroaromatic explosives (0–50 nM). Fluorescence spectra of sensors 4 and 5 (40 μM) in aqueous solution (H2O/THF (7:3, v/v) exhibited weak emission bands at 511 and 561, respectively (λexc = 310 nm (4) and 327 nm (5)). Large spectral shift in emission profile of sensors 4 (64 nm) and 5 (114 nm) in comparison to that of sensor 3 is ascribed to inclusion of additional hydroxyl units attached to aromatic ring that increases their electronic density and π conjugation. Addition of electron donating hydroxyl unit/s induced intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) in sensors 4 and 5 (Fig. 3 A). Upon addition of increasing concentrations of TNT (0–50 nM), an efficient enhancement in emission of sensors 4 and 5 was noticed as depicted in Fig. 3B. The fluorescence quantum yields (Φ) of sensors 4 and 5 before and after the addition of TNT were calculated by following the reported procedure [56] to support the restriction of ICT and ESIPT processes. Fluorescein was used as a standard compound to estimate quantum yield. It was noticed that the quantum yields of sensors 4 and 5 were increased from 0.58 to 0.73 and 0.55 to 0.69 after the addition of TNT. The results illustrate the restriction of the ICT and ESIPT processes that consequently lead to the enhanced fluorescence emission of sensors.

Maximum 75 and 63-folds enhancement in emission of sensors 4 and 5 with potential blue shift of 39 and 17 nm, respectively was achieved at maximal 50 nM TNT (Fig. 3). Significant fluorescence enhancement response with hypsochromic shift was due to disturbance in ICT process of sensors 4 and 5 as a result of π-π and CH-π interaction between sensor and TNT. It provides structural rigidity in sensor that ultimately leads to fluorescence emission enhancement. In addition to this, when TNT solution (10 nM) was added to sensors 4 and 5 (500 nM), sensor 4 displayed bright emission. Similarly, the dark blue color of sensor 5 also showed bright emission under UV (365 nm) upon addition of TNT (10 nM) (inset; Fig. 3D).

Further, the binding association of sensors 4 and 5 for TNT were determined through the Benesi–Hildebrand plots (Fig. 3). The Benesi–Hildebrand plots reveal excellent linear response between sensor and TNT. Excellent linear fluorometric behavior of sensors 4 and 5 in the presence of various concentrations of TNT (0–50 nM) make them credible source for quantification of TNT explosive. The association constant (Ka) of sensors 4 and 5 for TNT were found to be 5.17 × 107 and 5.02 × 107 M−1, respectively.

Ka values clearly indicate the strong association of sensors 4 and 5 with TNT explosive.Lesser Ka value of sensor 5 for TNT in comparison to that of sensor 4 is probably due to its relatively weak ICT process that corresponds to intramolecular hydrogen bonding between two neighboring hydroxyl units in sensor 5. In this context, sensor 5 was expected to be less sensitive towards TNT as compared to sensor 4. Sensitivity of sensors 4 and 5 for detection of TNT was calculated through 3 σ/S and was found to be 5.0 and 7.0 nM, respectively. As anticipated, sensor 5 was marginally less sensitive possibly due to the above mentioned reasoning. Strong association and highly sensitive response of sensors 4 and 5 for TNT is compared with previously reported TNT sensors and summarized in Table 3 . To the best of our knowledge, no fluorescence enhancement based TNT sensor has been reported yet for the sensitive detection of TNT explosive at nanomolar level preferably in aqueous medium.

Further, fluorescence response of sensors 4 and 5 were also accomplished towards continual addition of remaining nitroaromatic explosives including DNT, TNP, NP, NA, and DNB. Resulting emission spectra of sensors 4 and 5 with nitroaromatic explosives displayed little enhancement at 511 and 561 nm, respectively (Figs. S14 and S15). Upon addition of increasing concentrations of DNT (0–100 nM) to sensors 4 and 5 (40 μM), an enhancement (7 and 4-folds) in their fluorescence emission was observed as presented in Figs. S14A and S15A. Calculated detection limit (44 nM (4) and 21 nM (5)) and binding association values (2.16 × 104 (4) and 2.7 × 103 M−1 (5)) of sensors for DNT are relatively less in comparison to that for TNT. Next, sensors 4 and 5 displayed insignificant fluorescence enhancement response towards continuous addition of TNP, NA, NP, and DNB (0–100 nM) with less Ka values and low detection limits. Depending upon the calculated Ka values and detection limits, fluorescence enhancement response of sensors 4 and 5 towards nitroaromatic explosives follows the order; TNT > DNT > TNP > NA > NP > DNB. Association constants (Ka) values of sensors 4 and 5 along with LOD for nitroaromatic explosives are given in Tables S2 and S3, respectively. Better sensitivity and high binding constant (Ka) value make sensors 4 and 5 highly selective for detection of TNT. Moreover, % enhancement in emission of sensors 4 and 5 due to TNT explosive and other targeted nitroaromatics also ensure the excellent selectivity of sensors towards TNT (Fig. 4 A and C). Distinguished enhancement efficiency of > 94% and > 91% (calculated through I0/I) in emissions of sensors 4 and 5, respectively for TNT is associated with strong complexation between sensor and TNT that blocks the ICT and ESIPT. Less % enhancement efficiency of sensors 4 and 5 with remaining nitroaromatics is due to their insignificant complexation. Selective sensing response of sensors 4 and 5 (40 μM) for TNT was further justified through the Benesi–Hildebrand 3D column. Fig. 4 suggested that a linear increase in emission intensity of sensors 4 and 5 was observed only in the presence of increasing concentration of TNT (0–100 nM). The fluorescence spectra remained unaffected in presence of all other considered NACs. Similar pattern was observed through colorimetric analysis in which both sensors displayed selective colorimetric identification of TNT under UV light (365 nm) (Inset; Fig. 4B and D). The strongest complexation of sensors 4 and 5 with TNT explosive were also validated through UV–Vis. spectroscopy under the same experimental conditions as opted for fluorescence studies. No change or shifting in the absorption wavelength of sensors 4 (351 nm) and 5 (391 nm) was noticed in presence of nitroaromatic explosives including TNP, NA, NP, and DNB (50 nM). However, in presence of TNT (50 nM), a completely new absorption band of sensors 4 and 5 was appeared at 486 and 498 nm, respectively (Fig. S16A and C).

These spectral changes are clear indication of selective complexation of sensors with TNT that correspond to the restriction in ICT and ESIPT of sensors in the presence of TNT. Further, to the solution of sensors 4 and 5 (500 nM), upon addition of different nitroaromatic explosives (10 μM), only TNT showed obvious color change while other NACs didn’t alter the yellow color of sensors 4 and 5 (Inset; Fig. S16A and C). Next, the UV–Vis. spectral changes of sensors 4 and 5 (40 μM) upon titration with different concentrations of TNT (0–50 nM) were recorded as shown in Fig. S16. Free sensors 4 and 5 (40 μM) displayed one clear broad absorption band at 351 and 391 nm, respectively. During the incremental addition of TNT (0–50 nM), a completely new absorption band of sensor 4 (40 μM) appeared at 486 nm followed by hyperchromic shift at 351 and 386 nm. Further, continuous addition of TNT (0–50 nM) to sensor 5 (40 μM) leads to the appearance of new absorption band at 498 nm along with significant hyperchromic shift at 391 and 498 nm. Further, ratiometric linear fit plots presented in Fig. S17 show excellent binding association between sensors and TNT explosive. Binding values of sensors 4 and 5 with TNT were determined to be 5.09 × 107 and 4.13 × 107 M−1, respectively. Hence, these results support selectivity of sensor 3 towards TNP and sensors 4 and 5 towards TNT. These spectral results indicated the disturbance in both excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) pathways in sensors upon addition of TNT explosive.

Favorable selectivity of sensors 4 and 5 towards TNT was also validated through interaction energies of sensors complexes with targeted nitroaromatics through Gaussian 09 program using B3LYP/6-31G(d) methodology. Optimized complexes of sensors with considered nitroaromatics are given in Figs. S18 and S19. TNT@4 exhibited more interaction as compared to all other complexes with NACs that shows favorable selectivity of sensor 4 towards TNT. Interaction energies of TNT@4, DNT@4, NP@4, TNP@4, NA@4, and DNB@4 are −12.49, −6.26, −5.37, −4.11, −3.55, and −1.26 kcal/mol, respectively. Calculated interaction energies (Eint) for sensor 4 against nitroaromatics are in the order of TNT > DNT > NP > TNP > NA > DNB. Similarly, interaction energies provided by TNT@5, DNT@5, NP@5, TNP@5, NA@5, and DNB@5 are −11.33, −10.41, −5.13, −4.07, −2.66, and −2.24 kcal/mol, respectively. These results elicit the strongest interaction in TNT@5 in comparison to the remaining nitroaromatics. Binding order of sensor 5 towards targeted NACs is as follows; TNT > DNT > NP > TNP > NA > DNB. Hence, these theoretical calculations demonstrate the selectivity of sensors 4 and 5 towards TNT that is in full agreement with emission and absorption studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic approach towards selective detection of TNP by sensor 3
ce:section.ce:para: In reference to the schematic illustration presented in Fig. 5 A, the highly sensitive sensing potential of sensor 3 for TNP is based on the electrostatic interaction between sensor 3 with TNP. The phenolic unit of TNP is highly acidic owing to electron withdrawing effect of its three nitro (–NO2) groups. Further, Low pka value (0.38) of TNP defines its acidic nature. Therefore, the highly acidic phenolic group of TNP interacts efficiently with amine unit (basic) of sensor 3. Such electrostatic interactions hinder the common non-radiative decay pathways i.e. photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Intramolecular electron transfer (i.e. PET) in lone sensor 3 reduces its energy profile and ultimately makes it less emissive. Hindered intramolecular electronic rotations (i.e. PET) of sensor 3 in complex state with TNP induce aggregation and rigidity in the sensor that ultimately enhance fluorescence emission. The interaction of picrate ion with amino group are well elaborated by Bedanta and co-workers where an amino group of curcumin-tryptophan conjugate develops a selective electrostatic interaction with picrate ion [37]. Strong electrostatic interaction between sensor 3 and TNP was supported by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements as displayed in Fig. 5. The DLS has been an effective tool to determine the best possible interaction between a sensor and analyte through size determination. Average particle size of 5 mL sensor 3 (1.0 × 10-3 mol/L) was 91 nm (PDI = 0.313) and 459 nm (PDI = 0.984) before and after the addition of TNP (2 × 10-2 mol/L), respectively (Fig. 5B and C). Such significant aggregation in size and PDI value is responsible for enhancement in fluorescence of sensor 3 and is generally termed as aggregation induced emission (AIE). Further, DLS measurements revealed that introduction of TNP to sensor 3 induced prominent changes in zeta potential (ζ) values of sensor 3. Initially, sensor 3 displayed positive potential (ζ) value of about + 15.4 mV that changes to −31.4 mV after addition of TNP (Fig. S20). Significant change in zeta potential (ζ) value of sensor 3 is a strong indication of the electrostatic interaction between sensor and TNP.

Electrostatic interaction between sensor 3 and TNP was justified through 1H NMR titration experiments in the presence of TNP in DMSO‑d6 (Fig. 6 ). 1H NMR spectrum of sensor 3 shows that peak at δ 5.6472 ppm corresponds to the amine (NH2) proton (Ha). Signals observed at 6.683, 6.806, 7.115, 7.140, 7.370, 7.458, ppm are equivalent to other aromatic protons Hb, Hc, Hd, He, Hf, and Hg, respectively. It was noticed that signals of amine (–NH2) proton (Ha) and other aromatic protons (Hb, Hc, Hd, He, Hf, and Hg) notably shifted upfield in the presence of TNP in comparison to 1H NMR spectrum of lone sensor 3 in DMSO‑d6. Addition of 0.5 equiv TNP to sensor 3 solution in DMSO‑d6 prompted a significant upfield shift in amino protons (Ha) signal from δ 5.6472 to 5.6408 ppm. Other aromatic protons (Hb, Hc, Hd, He, Hf, and Hg) displayed upfield shift from δ 6.682–6.677, 6.805–6.804, 7.114–7.113, 7.130–7.111, 7.360–350, and 7.457–7.451 ppm, respectively (Fig. 6). Addition of 1.0 equiv of TNP to sensor 3 further shifted the amine protons (Ha) from 5.6408 to 5.6401 ppm and maximum shifting of amine protons (Ha) signal (5.6401–5.6378 ppm) occurred at the addition of 2.0 equiv of TNP. In addition to this, the aromatic protons (Hh) of TNP shifted downfield from δ 8.5858 to 8.5913 ppm that is clear evidence of electrostatic interaction between aromatic unit of TNP and sensor 3. 1H NMR titration results are summarized in Table S4. These experimental results further suggested that maximum interaction between sensor 3 and TNP was observed upon addition of 2.0 equiv TNP that confirms 1:2 stoichiometric associations between them.

Further, stoichiometric association of sensor 3 with TNP was also supported by Job’s plot. For this purpose, different mole fractions of TNP were gradually added to sensors 3 (40 µM) in aqueous media (100% H2O). Fig. S21 reveals that maximum fluorescence signal of sensor 3 was displayed at 0.6 mol fraction of TNP. Job’s plots at 0.6 mol fraction of TNP verify 1:2 binding stoichiometry between sensor 3 and TNP (Fig. S21). This binding stoichiometry is consistent with the outcomes of 1H NMR titration experiment (Fig. 6). Further, sensing mechanism was evaluated by density function theory (DFT) methodology through Gaussian 09 software [55]. Geometries and frontier molecular orbital (FMO) analysis of sensor 3 and TNP explosive were optimized at B3LYP/6-31G (d) level of theory. Fig. 7 reveals that in HOMO-LUMO surfaces of sensor 3, HOMO was localized on the amine and fluorenone rings of sensor, and LUMO was dispersed over the complete fluorenone region (including carbonyl group) which accounts for excellent electron transfer (PET) within the sensor. However, in LUMO level of sensor + TNP complex, electron density is restricted to fluorenone unit. This phenomenon is attributed to disruption in non-radiative pathway (PET) of sensor 3 due to electrostatic interaction of sensor 3 with TNP. HOMO-LUMO (H-L) energy gap of sensor 3 was calculated as 2.19 eV. Alteration in H-L gap for complexes reflects the extent of their interaction.

The HOMO, LUMO surfaces of TNP@3 are presented in Fig. 7. While HOMO-LUMO surfaces of all remaining complexes are given in Fig. S22. After interaction with nitroaromatics, noticeable decrease in H-L energy gap of TNP@3 complex revealed increase in interaction probability. In TNP@3, H-L energy gap was calculated to be 0.48 eV while H-L gap in NP@3, DNT@3, TNT@3, NA@3 and DNB@3 were found as 2.15, 2.64, 2.72, 2.85, and 2.87 eV, respectively. Prominent change in H-L gap after complexation of sensor 3 with TNP is a clear indication of the strongest electrostatic interaction as compared to other targeted NACs.

The reversibility and reusability of any sensor provide them an advantage for selective sensing of specific nitroaromatic explosive. Electrostatic interaction between sensor 3 and picrate ion can also be authenticated through the study of salt screening or reversibility tests [62]. In first experiment, the emission intensity of sensor 3 was efficiently increased at 447 nm upon addition of TNP and the colorless solution of sensor 3 produced strong blue emission when irradiated at 365 nm under UV (Fig. 8 A and S23). Reversibility of sensor 3 to TNP was observed when concentrated NaNO3 solution (0.2 M) was added to solution of 3-TNP complex. Interestingly, addition of NaNO3 to 3 + TNP solution led to the regeneration of original emission intensity at 447 nm and restored original color of sensor 3. It predicts that interaction between sensor and TNP was hampered by the addition of salt. Sodium ion from concentrated NaNO3 solution could form a sodium picrate making picrate ions unavailable to interact with protonated amino group of sensor 3. This reversibility test confirms the electrostatic interaction between sensor 3 and TNP. Recovery of original fluorescence emission and color of the solution demonstrates that fluorescence enhancement of sensor 3 occurs due to strong electrostatic interaction between hydroxyl unit of TNP and amino group of sensor 3. Fig. S23 elucidates how reversibility cycle works through increase and decrease of emission intensity upon mixing of TNP and NaNO3 to sensor 3. In addition to this, reversibility experiments suggest that sensor 3 can act as a promising recyclable sensor for on-site detection of TNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic approach towards selective detection of TNT by sensors 4 and 5
ce:section.ce:para: Schiff base derivatives have attracted much attention in the field of sensing electron deficient substrates. Since these sensors possess nitrogen–oxygen electron rich system that can make an efficient interaction with electron deficient analyte, leading to variations in their photophysical properties. Herein, sensors 4 and 5 have two sections in their Schiff base-based structure. Fluorenone scaffold is regarded as the fluorophore while hydroxyl substituted aromatic unit attached through imine moiety (-C = N-) is referred as receptor. In sensor, additional hydroxyl/s (receptor) can induce electronic transitions with neighboring imine bond of fluorenone scaffold (fluorophore), leading to the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). The presence of –OH–-N type hydrogen bonding (H-bonding) and transfer of hydroxyl proton to adjacent imine nitrogen in sensors 4 and 5 signify the excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) process. However, after the interaction of TNT explosive with the receptor, the association of hydroxyl proton with TNT and complexation of TNT with sensor molecule restrict the ESIPT and ICT pathways. Meanwhile the –CH = N- isomerization is also suppressed. Restriction of these processes renders strong rigidity in sensors 4 and 5 that resulted in the emission enhancement in the presence of TNT explosive. It is anticipated that complexation of TNT with sensors 4 and 5 occur through their π-π, CH-π, or CH-NO2 interaction as illustrated in Fig. 9 A. It is estimated that in the excited state of sensors 4 and 5, the ICT and ESIPT processes provide electronic transitions from receptor to fluorophore unit that is probably due to the higher energy HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) level of the receptor as compared to the HOMO of fluorophore. After binding with TNT, HOMO of the bound receptor is expected to become lower in energy as compared to the HOMO of excited fluorophore (Fig. S24). Stability in the HOMO level of the bound receptor inhibits ICT and ESIPT process accompanied by inhibition of -C = N- isomerization (Fig. 9B). As a result, sensor molecule does not consume its absorbed energy to carry out these non-radiative processes that results in the elevation in radiative pathways, leading to the enhanced fluorescence emission. These assumptions were validated by the density function theory (DFT) calculations using Gaussian 09 software [55].

Fig. 7 reveals that in HOMO-LUMO surfaces of sensors 4 and 5, the HOMO was localized on the phenyl groups of the sensor units, and the LUMO was dispersed over fluorenone region. It corresponds to the excellent intramolecular charge transfer within the sensors due to higher HOMO energy level of receptor in comparison to the HOMO of fluorophore unit. However, in LUMO level of sensor + TNT complex, the electron density is confined to fluorophore units of sensors 4 and 5 that is probably due to lowering in HOMO energy level of receptor. This phenomenon leads to hamper the ICT and ESIPT of sensors 4 and 5 as a result of their interaction with TNT explosive. Further, energy gaps i.e. HOMO-LUMO gaps of sensors 4 and 5 were calculated as 2.41 and 2.28 eV, respectively. The HOMO, LUMO surfaces of TNT@4 and TNT@5 are presented in Fig. 7.

While HOMO and LUMO surfaces of all remaining complexes are provided in Fig. S25. After interaction with TNT, noticeable decrease in H-L energy gap of sensor-TNT complex revealed increase in interaction probability. Calculated H-L gap values for TNT@4 is 0.92 eV which is the least calculated gap among targeted NACs. Similarly, H-L gap values for TNT@5 was found to be 3.39 which is the lowest calculated value among all considered NACs. All calculated H-L gap values are presented in Table S5. Hence, results obtained through DFT studies also support the proposed mechanism and excellent selectivity of sensors 4 and 5 towards TNT. Such favorable selectivity and excellent sensitivity of sensors towards TNT was also confirmed through Jobs’ plots. Results presented in Fig. S26 explain 1:1 binding stoichiometry of sensors 4 and 5 with TNT explosive which account for the excellent sensitivity of sensors 4 and 5 towards detection of TNT explosive.

Favored binding stoichiometry of sensors with TNT was supported by dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis through size determination. Average size of 1.0 × 10-3 mol/L solution of sensors 4 (PDI = 0.451) and 5 (PDI = 0.366) was calculated to be 122 and 138 nm before addition of TNT explosive (1.0 × 10-2 mol/L) (Fig. S27A and C). A strong increase in particle size was noticed for the complex of TNT with 4 (PDI = 0.971) and 5 (PDI = 0.982) and particle size were found to be 505 (4-TNT) and 625 nm (5-TNT). Therefore, this significant increase in particle size after addition of TNT favors the prominent complexation between sensor and TNT explosive.

Since sensing medium for detection of a specific nitroaromatic explosive commonly contains other interfering compounds. Therefore, anti-interference emission study was performed to investigate selectivity of sensor 3 towards TNP and sensors 4 and 5 towards TNT. In this regard, TNP (20 nM) solution was added to sensor 3 (40 μM) in the presence of 100 equivalents of other interferences including nitroaromatic explosives, glucose, galactose, fructose, and commonly used organic solvents as shown in Fig. S28. Results presented in Fig. S28 show that presence of even high concentrations (100 equiv.) of interferences render no influence on selective detection of TNP by sensor 3. Further, the effect of interferences to sensitivity of sensors 4 and 5 towards TNT is presented in Fig. S29. No notable change in the fluorescence response of sensors 4 and 5 towards TNT was noticed in the presence of interferences. These results provide excellent selectivity to sensor 3 for detection of TNP and sensors 4 and 5 for detection of TNT explosive. Excellent selectivity of these sensors is crucial and provides advantage to employ them in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TNT and TNP responsive test kits
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, solid supported emitting materials have attracted considerable attention in the field of fluorescent sensors and organic light emitting diodes (display materials) [63]. High emission in solid state is basic requirement to fabricate device for applications in organic electronics (OLEDs). Keeping this in view, significant fluorescence enhancement response of sensors 3, 4 and 5 towards

nitroaromatic explosive in solution prompted us to develop efficient solid supported emitters for the convenient detection of TNP and TNT explosive. In this regard, explosive responsive test kits were prepared by coating sensors 3, 4 and 5 (1 × 10-3 M) in THF on the surface of paper strips followed by drying in air. Dried paper strips immersed in the solution (1 × 10-3 M) of sensors 3, 4 and 5 displayed no fluorescence emission when irradiated under UV (365 nm). However, sensor 3 displayed bright emission under 365 nm radiations when spot of TNP solution (1 × 10-2 M) was placed on to sensor 3 adsorbed strip. Similarly, sensors 4 and 5 displayed no emission under UV (365 nm). A spot-on sensor’s coated strips was marked by capillary tube containing solution of TNT explosive (1 × 10-2 M) and then dried. Dried strips marked with TNT explosive responded with significant fluorescence emission. Non emissive sensor’s coated strips were turned to bright green color wherever solution of TNT (1 × 10-2 M) explosive touches the strips of both sensors 4 and 5 (Figs. 10 and S30). However, dried paper strips of sensor 3, 4, and 5 displayed no emission under UV when other competing NACs were applied to them. Hence, explosive responsive test strips of sensor 3 for TNP and sensors 4 and 5 for TNT are a convenient sensing tool and can be used for the rapid on-site recognition of these explosives with high sensitivity and selectivity. Based on good paper strip response by sensors, an experiment was pursued for the on-site detection of TNT to validate the practical applicability of 4 and 5. In this regard, silica gel TLC plates (thickness: 210–270 μm) soaked with THF solution (1 × 10-3 M) of sensors 4 and 5 were dried in oven at 30 °C for 1 h. The TNT contaminated thumb impression was applied to sensors’ coated TLC plates (Figs. 11 and S31). Consequently, the fluorescence of sensors 4 and 5 turned on and was distinctly observed under UV (λ = 365 nm). Thus, prepared sensor’s coated TLC strips can be integrated as efficient test kits for the on-site detection of nitroaromatic explosives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of explosives in industrial effluents
ce:section.ce:para: Practical application of developed sensors for quantification of TNP and TNT was interrogated in industrial wastewater through spike/recovery test that was executed under above mentioned experimental parameters. Industrial wastewater samples were collected from Pakistan Ordinance Factory (POF), Havelian, Abbottabad, KPK, Pakistan and were filtered first to eliminate undesired particles. To determine TNP and TNT in the industrial wastewater, different concentrations of TNP and TNT explosives (10 and 50 nM) were spiked in the industrial samples. TNP spiked solutions were added into solutions of sensor 3 (40 μM) while TNT spiked industrial solutions were added into solutions of sensors 4 and 5 (40 μM) and fluorescence emission spectra were obtained as shown in Fig. S32. It was observed that the fluorescence emission enhancement response for these samples relates the pattern that has been noticed for the laboratory analytes. Fig. S32 reveal that spiked TNP and TNT (10 and 50 nM) was recovered > 90% Recovery determination of spiked TNP by sensor 3 and TNT by sensors 4 and 5 are given in Tables 4 and S6-S7.

In summary, three fluorenone based sensors 3, 4, and 5 were rationally synthesized and applied for detection of nitroaromatic explosives. Structural characterizations of sensors 3, 4, and 5 were performed through 1H and 13C NMR and their sensing studies were investigated through fluorescence and UV–Visible spectroscopies. Sensor 3 was employed for efficient fluorescence sensing of TNP with sensitivity level down to nanomolar level (0.5 nM) that is due to electrostatic interaction between them. Sensors 4 and 5 detected TNT explosive in aqueous medium through selective fluorescence emission enhancement response with LOD 5.0 and 7.0 nM, respectively. Fluorescence “enhancement” response of sensors 4 and 5 towards TNT is due to blockage of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) processes. Moreover, sensor 3 displayed selective colorimetric detection of TNP while sensors 4 and 5 were exploited for selective colorimetric detection of TNT explosive. Explosive responsive test kits were prepared for the detection of TNP by sensor 3 and TNT by sensors 4 and 5. Additionally, identification and quantification of TNP and TNT were executed in industrial samples through spike/recovery test. To the best of our knowledge, fluorescence enhancement based small fluorenone sensors are developed first time for the determination of TNP and TNT in aqueous medium.
